---
title: "About"
---

I'm a public-health data professional and GIS developer moving further into
spatial epidemiology. My degree is in geography, and I've spent over a decade
building and analyzing large health and geospatial datasets — in public-health
surveillance, healthcare operations, and federal hazard modeling. The current
focus is applying that to epidemiologic problems: surveillance data,
environmental exposure, and the spatial and temporal structure that ordinary
regression tends to ignore. I'm based in Centennial, Colorado.

## Now

I'm finishing an MPH in Epidemiology at the University of Memphis. My thesis is
quantitative: a Bayesian spatiotemporal study of West Nile virus mosquito
surveillance across California, looking at how environmental covariates,
surveillance effort, and space-time patterns relate to infection rates in
mosquito pools. Because the surveillance data is access-restricted, I built the
validation first — a simulation framework that checks whether candidate models
actually recover a known effect at the stated uncertainty before any of them
touch the real data.

Professionally, I develop GIS and risk-modeling software as a contractor
supporting FEMA's Hazus natural-hazard loss-estimation program. Day to day that
means Python, C#/C++, and SQL Server work against ArcGIS-based data and modeling
workflows — data engineering, hazard and loss logic, and the validation that
goes with changing a model people rely on.

Before this I worked as a GIS analyst in healthcare (DaVita Kidney Care) and, at
a Colorado health department during the pandemic, built COVID-19 surveillance
databases, ETL pipelines, and public dashboards.

## How I work

A few things I keep coming back to:

- **Treat surveillance as data with a process behind it.** Where and how hard you
  look shapes what you find. Detection and effort belong in the model, not in a
  footnote.
- **Validate before you trust.** New spatial code gets checked against
  established implementations (PySAL, R packages); new models get checked on
  simulated data with a known answer before being turned loose on real data.
- **Make it reproducible.** Pipelines over one-off scripts; version control;
  environments someone else can rebuild.
- **Say what the analysis can and can't support.** I'd rather state a limitation
  plainly than round a result up.

## Interests

My work sits across spatial and computational epidemiology, environmental
health, public-health GIS, and applied statistics. I'm particularly interested
in vector-borne disease surveillance, spatial cluster detection, and the methods
problems that come up when surveillance data is sparse, uneven, or collected for
something other than research.

## Contact

- Email: [ross@rwardrup.com](mailto:ross@rwardrup.com)
- GitHub: [github.com/minorsecond](https://github.com/minorsecond)
- LinkedIn: [linkedin.com/in/robert-wardrup](https://www.linkedin.com/in/robert-wardrup/)
- [Curriculum vitae](cv.qmd)
